record_office_terminology.land_records: Recorder of Deeds (deeds, mortgages, liens, UCC filings -- single office for all of DC)
record_office_terminology.probate: DC Superior Court, Probate Division (wills, estates, guardianships; formerly DC Supreme Court)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: DC Superior Court, Marriage Bureau (licenses from 1811; no waiting period or residency requirement historically)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1874 (births and deaths; DC Board of Health began registration; marriages from 1811 at the court)

burned_county_authority: DC Archives (formerly Washingtoniana Division, DC Public Library) -- DC is not a burned jurisdiction; the British burning of Washington in August 1814 destroyed the Capitol, White House, and some government buildings, but most local court and land records survived; overall record survival is good from the 1790s forward
administrative_notes: The District of Columbia is a unique federal jurisdiction -- not a state, not a county. Created by the Residence Act of 1790 from land ceded by Maryland and Virginia. The Virginia portion (including Alexandria) was retroceded in 1846. Georgetown (incorporated 1789) and Washington City (incorporated 1802) were separate municipalities until consolidated with Washington County into a single territorial government in 1871. DC lost home rule in 1874 and was governed by appointed commissioners until the Home Rule Act of 1973. Researchers must distinguish between NATIONAL repositories in DC (NARA, Library of Congress) and DC-LOCAL records (Superior Court, DC Archives, Recorder of Deeds). For pre-1791 research, consult Maryland records (Prince George's and Charles counties) and Virginia records (Fairfax and Arlington -- though Arlington County was not separated until 1920 from Alexandria County). Slavery was legal in DC until the Compensated Emancipation Act of April 16, 1862, which freed approximately 3,100 enslaved persons and paid slaveholders up to $300 per person. DC had one of the largest free Black communities in the antebellum United States. After the Civil War, DC became a major destination for formerly enslaved persons from Virginia, Maryland, and the broader South.

burned_counties_summary.notes: DC is not a burned jurisdiction. The 1814 British burning damaged federal buildings but most local records survived.

core_repositories.source_name: National Archives and Records Administration (NARA) -- DC-Specific Holdings
core_repositories.repository: National Archives, Washington, DC / Archives II, College Park, MD
core_repositories.description: While NARA serves the entire nation, it holds DC-SPECIFIC records including: DC federal court records, DC Compensated Emancipation Act petitions (1862), Freedmen's Bureau DC office records (1865-1872), DC draft registrations, and records of the DC territorial and commissioner governments. The DC-related holdings are distinct from the national collections.

The official archives of the District of Columbia government. Holds local government records including Board of Health records, almshouse records, public school records, building permits, tax assessment records, police records, and records of the DC commissioners and city council. Essential for DC-local research as distinct from federal records at NARA.

Major manuscript repository for DC local history. Holdings include family papers, business records, neighborhood histories, photographs, maps, and Washington city directories from 1822. The Kiplinger Research Library has published genealogies, local histories, and DC periodicals. Strong collections on Georgetown, Capitol Hill, and African American neighborhoods.

The nation's premier repository for African American history and culture. Holdings include manuscripts, personal papers, organizational records, photographs, and printed materials documenting the African American experience from the colonial era to the present. Critical for researching DC's free Black community, freedmen's experience, and African American institutions. Includes records of Howard University (est. 1867) and its role in African American education.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including DC deed books (from 1792), probate records, marriage records (from 1811), church records, cemetery records, and city directories. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively at the DC Recorder of Deeds, DC Superior Court, and local churches. Key collections include DC marriages, DC land records, and DC church records.

familysearch_collections.source_name: FamilySearch Wiki -- District of Columbia Genealogy
familysearch_collections.description: Comprehensive research guide covering DC's unique jurisdictional history, record availability by type and time period, and links to digitized microfilm. Explains the distinction between DC-local and federal records. Covers Georgetown, Washington City, and Washington County records before the 1871 consolidation.

newspapers_and_periodicals.source_name: Chronicling America -- District of Columbia Newspapers
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic DC newspapers including the Evening Star (1852-1981, the dominant DC daily for over a century), National Intelligencer (1800-1870, the paper of record for early Washington), and numerous other titles. Legal notices, obituaries, advertisements for enslaved persons (pre-1862), and community news are invaluable for genealogical research.

The Washington Afro-American (and related African American newspapers including The Bee, The Colored American, and New National Era edited by Frederick Douglass) documented DC's African American community from the 1870s. Obituaries, social news, church announcements, and community events not covered in mainstream white newspapers. Essential for African American genealogy in DC.

military_records.source_name: DC Military Records -- NARA / DC Archives
military_records.description: DC militia rolls from 1802; Civil War records including defenses of Washington (extensive ring of forts protecting the capital), USCT regiments recruited in DC, and draft records. WWI and WWII draft registration cards for DC residents. Veterans' burial records at Congressional Cemetery (from 1807), Soldiers' Home National Cemetery, and Rock Creek Cemetery. DC Grand Army of the Republic (GAR) post records.

land_and_property.source_name: DC Recorder of Deeds -- Land Records from 1792
land_and_property.description: Deed books from 1792 for all DC real property. Includes original Washington City lots (L'Enfant Plan), Georgetown properties, and Washington County (rural DC) land. Early deeds often describe property with detailed metes and bounds. FamilySearch has digitized many early deed books. Tax assessment records at DC Archives complement the land records. Property records are essential for tracking families in DC, where land ownership patterns reflect racial and economic segregation.

vital_records.source_name: DC Vital Records -- Department of Health
vital_records.repository: DC Department of Health, Vital Records Division
vital_records.description: Birth and death certificates from 1874 (DC Board of Health began registration). Marriage licenses from 1811 at DC Superior Court. Pre-1874 vital events must be reconstructed from church records, newspaper notices, cemetery records, and Board of Health burial permits (from 1855). DC vital records are centralized -- there are no separate county offices. Older records may be at DC Archives.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Various congregations / Historical Society of Washington, DC / FamilySearch
church_and_cemetery.description: DC's church records are exceptionally rich and diverse. Major congregations with early records: Christ Church Georgetown (Episcopal, 1794), St. Patrick's Catholic (1794), St. John's Episcopal Lafayette Square (1816), Foundry Methodist (1814), First Baptist (1802), Metropolitan AME (1838), Fifteenth Street Presbyterian (1841). The Catholic Archdiocese of Washington maintains sacramental registers. Many records are microfilmed on FamilySearch. For pre-1874 vital events, church records are often the only source.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: DC Cemeteries -- Congressional Cemetery, Rock Creek, Oak Hill, and Others
church_and_cemetery.description: Congressional Cemetery (est. 1807) is one of the oldest national cemeteries; burial records include members of Congress, military figures, and ordinary citizens. Rock Creek Cemetery (1719, one of the oldest in DC), Oak Hill Cemetery (Georgetown, 1849), Glenwood Cemetery (1854), and Mount Olivet Cemetery (Catholic, 1858). Harmony Cemetery (African American, 1869) was relocated in 1960 -- records at DC Archives. Columbian Harmony Cemetery records document African American burials. FindAGrave has extensive DC coverage.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: Afro-American Historical and Genealogical Society (AAHGS) -- National Headquarters
research_guides_and_societies.description: Founded in 1977 and headquartered in Washington, DC, AAHGS is the leading organization for African American genealogy. Publishes the AAHGS Journal, hosts conferences, and maintains research resources. DC chapter focuses on DC-area African American families. Essential contact for researchers working on African American genealogy in DC.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: Library of Congress -- Local History and Genealogy Reading Room
research_guides_and_societies.description: While the Library of Congress serves the nation, its Local History and Genealogy Reading Room holds DC city directories, local histories, published genealogies, and maps. The Prints and Photographs Division has historic DC images. The Manuscript Division holds papers of DC residents and organizations. The Geography and Map Division has Sanborn fire insurance maps for DC (ward-level detail of buildings and occupants).

historical_counties_extinct.source_name: Pre-DC Jurisdictions -- Georgetown, Washington City, Washington County, and Alexandria (pre-1846)
historical_counties_extinct.repository: DC Archives / Historical Society of Washington, DC / Virginia State Archives (for Alexandria)
historical_counties_extinct.description: Before the 1871 consolidation, DC consisted of three separate jurisdictions: Georgetown (incorporated 1789 under Maryland, became part of DC in 1791), Washington City (incorporated 1802 within the federal district), and Washington County (the rural area outside both cities). Each maintained separate records. Georgetown's records predate DC and include Maryland colonial-era documents. The Virginia portion of DC (including Alexandria) was retroceded in 1846 -- post-1846 Alexandria records are at Virginia State Archives. Pre-1791 records for the DC area are in Maryland's Prince George's County and Charles County records.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: DC Compensated Emancipation Act Records (1862) -- NARA
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: The DC Compensated Emancipation Act of April 16, 1862 freed approximately 3,100 enslaved persons -- nine months before the Emancipation Proclamation. Slaveholder petitions name each enslaved person with age, physical description, and valuation. Supplementary petitions were filed by formerly enslaved persons claiming freedom. These records are digitized and searchable at NARA. Emancipation Day (April 16) is a DC holiday.

Documents the 1838 sale of 272 enslaved persons by Maryland Jesuits to Louisiana plantations. The Georgetown Memory Project has identified descendants. The archive includes Jesuit plantation records, correspondence, sacramental records, and financial documents. A critical resource for tracing African American families connected to Jesuit institutions in the DC/Maryland region.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Freedmen's Bureau DC Office Records -- NARA / FamilySearch
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands records for the DC sub-district (1865-1872). Includes labor contracts, school reports, marriage registers, hospital records (Freedmen's Hospital, later Howard University Hospital), and correspondence documenting formerly enslaved persons transitioning to freedom in DC. FamilySearch has digitized and indexed many Freedmen's Bureau records.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: DC Naturalization Records -- NARA / DC Superior Court
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records from DC courts (Circuit Court, Criminal Court, Supreme Court, and their successor DC Superior Court) from the early 1800s. DC attracted immigrants from many countries due to its role as the national capital -- diplomatic staff, government employees, domestic workers, and tradespeople. Records at NARA include declarations of intention, petitions for naturalization, and certificates. DC's immigrant communities included Irish (especially Swampoodle neighborhood), German, Italian, Chinese (Chinatown from 1880s), and Ethiopian (from 1970s) populations.
